text: The surgeon uses an applied medical key files trocar to enter the right retro rectus space, preparing for further dissection and repair of the incisional hernia.
The surgeon sweeps the fibril fatty areolar tissue away from the posterior sheath, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
Under direct visualization, the surgeon places two robotic ports into the expanded surgical field.
The initial five-millimeter port is upsized to an eight-millimeter robotic port under direct visualization.
The surgeon upsizes the epigastric eight-millimeter robotic port to a twelve-millimeter port to accommodate the placement of a heavyweight mesh.
The surgeon dissects towards the linea semilinare, revealing a large neurovascular bundle within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the entire retrorectus space, preparing the area for the upcoming transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.
The camera view shifts from the right lower quadrant to the right upper quadrant, providing a visual overview of the anatomical area.
The surgeon begins the transversus abdominis release (TAR) by making an incision in the posterior lamella of the internal oblique muscle.

The surgeon identifies a medial neurovascular bundle and proceeds to divide it. Following this, the posterior lamella of the internal oblique is incised, and the transverse abdominus muscle is divided to access the pre-transverse salus fascia plane.
The surgeon attempts to separate the transverse salus fascia from the transversus abdominis muscle, revealing the anatomical relationship between these structures.

The surgeon accidentally creates a small hole in the peritoneum, causing CO₂ pressure to equalize. They attempt to contain the CO₂ by pinching the hole, while a significant space is already developed in the retrorectal area.
The surgeon continues the transversus abdominis release (TAR) caudally, transitioning to the portion where the muscle becomes more aponeurotic.
The surgeon demonstrates the ETAP technique, where the transverse abdominus muscle visibly separates more easily from the transverse salus fascia.

The surgeon performs a complete transverse abdominus release, dissecting towards the hernia sac. The dissection progresses under the diaphragm and continues below the hernia sac in the right groin area.
The surgeon divides the aponeurotic portion of the posterior sheath, located medial to the linea semilinearis, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon cuts the posterior sheath at the medial edge, close to the linea semilunaris.
The surgeon navigates around the area of the hernia sac, with visible fat located below the hernia sac level, progressing into the right lower quadrant.
The assistant applies pressure on the hernia, while the surgeon flips the scope to begin working around the neck of the hernia, focusing on the surrounding tissue.
In the ETEP approach, the surgeon carefully manipulates the hernia sac from the outside, attempting to reduce the sac and its contents without entering the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon is at the neck of the hernia sac, attempting to create a plane between the hernia sac and surrounding structures to reduce it. The surgeon encounters difficulty finding a clear plane due to the presence of a neopartineum rather than a substantial hernia sac.
Within the cavity, the surgeon attempts to separate muscle tissue from the hernia sac, which is visible due to the hernia occurring post-partial nephrectomy.
The surgeon manipulates the hernia sac, revealing a very thin piece of peritoneum. Despite attempts to preserve the hernia sac, it eventually enters the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon attempts to find a layer to peel the extremely thin hernia sac away from the hernia walls. After assessing the difficulty, the surgeon decides to open up the hernia sac.
The surgeon observes periclonic fat, with the right colon visibly protruding into the hernia sac.

The surgeon reduces the colonic contents from the hernia sac while the patient is in the lateral tibialis position. The colon appears tethered within the sac, but the adhesions are minimal, allowing for relatively easy dissection and removal.
The surgeon uses a wristed robotic instrument to maneuver around the corners of the surgical field, identifying areas where the tissue is tethered and snipping a few adhesive bands to release them.
The video shows bare muscle striations in the background, indicating minimal presence of a hernia sac.
The surgeon divides the neck of the hernia and proceeds with lateral dissection, having identified and removed the only adhesion within the sac.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral portion of the hernia, staying close to the peritoneum. The dissection progresses cautiously due to the unfamiliarity of the space.
The surgeon attempts to access the area just behind the right perirenal fascia, aiming to medially rotate the kidney while avoiding excessive lateral movement.
The surgeon mobilizes the neck of the hernia sac, navigating through fatty tissue near the right psoas muscle, aiming to identify nerves such as the ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue to reveal two distinct layers, successfully identifying a clear dissection plane.
The surgeon uses their left hand to retract the posterior perirenal fascia towards themselves, revealing the lateral conal fascia behind it.
The surgeon observes lateral tissue weakness but notes that there is no discrete defect, indicating that all tissue layers appear intact.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, avoiding the kidney and ureter by staying lateral. Remnants of the plane between the posterior perirenal fascia and the lateral conal fascia are visible.
The surgeon connects the dissection to the previously developed plane in the upper portion of the transversus abdominis release (TAR), ensuring continuity between the dissected areas.
The patient is positioned in a straight lateral decubitus position. The surgeon medially rotates the peritoneum and the colon, achieving exposure up to the medial edge of the psoas muscle.

The surgeon enters a pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen or upper flank, approaching the area where the kidney is located. Areolar tissue is visible, providing a clear path for dissection.

The psoas muscle becomes visible as the surgeon carefully dissects anterior to it, preserving the psoas fascia to avoid injury to nearby nerves, including the genitofemoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, iliohypogastric, and ilioinguinal nerves.
The surgeon encounters scar tissue from a previous partial nephrectomy while retracting the kidney medially with the left hand. A Weck clip from the prior procedure is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a wrist sit instrument to maneuver around the kidney, encountering a firm structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates through dense scar tissue, revealing areas of fatty tissue with uncertain underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies a white Weck clip while positioned very lateral to the lateralmost portion of the incisional hernia, indicating that the dissection may conclude around this area.
The surgeon extends the dissection laterally to ensure adequate overlap for the placement of a heavy weight mesh, considering the patient's high BMI and body habitus.
A small peritoneal hole is visible near the lateral portion of the liver, which is left in place.

The surgeon identifies a large defect measuring six centimeters in the cranial-caudal direction and eight centimeters laterally, located from the right pelvis up to the subdiaphragmatic space. The area shows lateral weakness but does not appear to be a true hernia.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal hole, identified as the neck of the hernia sac, using a 3-0 suture approximately nine inches long.
The surgeon identifies the main defect, which lacks a significant hernia sac, and uses an OV lock suture measuring 180 inches to address the area.
The surgeon lays out sutures to manage the slack of a long suture, progressively tightening them to close the visible defect.
The surgeon decreases the pneumoperitoneum to approximately eight millimeters of mercury pressure, ensuring the muscle tissue comes together without any visible tears.

The surgeon identifies a defect within two muscle layers, likely the transverse abdominis and internal oblique. Using the left hand to brace the tissue, the surgeon pulls the suture with the right hand to prevent it from sawing through the tissue.

The surgeon identifies a large hole beneath the external oblique muscle and places a 5mm port under direct vision into the space. A 19 French JP drain is threaded through the port into the cavity to prevent the formation of a large seroma.

The surgeon places heavy weight mesh with good overlap over the suture line, noting varying tension across the area. Medially and laterally, there is minimal tension, while the central part shows some tension. The surgeon runs another row of suture to alleviate the tension in the central region.
The surgeon places a second row of sutures to offload tension from the initial row, ensuring the mesh covers the weak lateral area despite the absence of a discrete defect.

The surgeon examines the space for mesh placement, with instruments positioned between the peritoneum and lateral conal fascia. Another visible space is between the transverse salus and transverse abdominus. The surgeon identifies the anterior superior iliac spine and iliac crest, planning to position the mesh slightly below the iliac crest level.

The surgeon measures the space for mesh placement, noting dimensions of approximately twenty-five centimeters long by twenty-six centimeters wide. Due to the heavyweight barred mesh not fitting through an eight millimeter port, the surgeon upsizes to a twelve millimeter port for insertion.

The surgeon measures the mesh to fit into the groove posteriorly, anterior to the psoas muscle. The cut edge of the posterior sheath is visible, and additional dissection is performed to ensure adequate caudal overlap of the mesh.

The surgeon upsizes the initial optical entry port to twelve millimeters for the placement of heavyweight mesh. The mesh is unrolled laparoscopically while retracting the kidney, which is noted to be unexpectedly heavy.

The surgeon tucks the posterior lateral edge of the mesh into the groove adjacent to the psoas muscle, ensuring a lateral overlap of approximately ten centimeters. A suture technique is employed to secure the long mesh piece effectively.
